summary: I.M. Pei (Ieoh Ming Pei, 1917–2019) was a Chinese-American architect who died at age 102, celebrated for his Late Modernist designs featuring bold geometric forms including pyramids, triangles, and cylinders. His most famous works include the Louvre Pyramid in Paris, the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in Cleveland, the East Building of the National Gallery of Art in Washington DC, the Museum of Islamic Art in Doha, and the Bank of China Tower in Hong Kong.
comprehensive_summary:
I.M. Pei (Ieoh Ming Pei, 1917–2019) was a Chinese-American architect who trained at the Harvard University Graduate School of Design and became one of the twentieth century's most celebrated modernist architects, dying at age 102. His work is characterized by bold geometric forms — particularly pyramids, triangles, and cylinders — often executed in glass and concrete.

His most famous work is the Louvre Pyramid in Paris, completed in 1989, which serves as the main public entrance to the Louvre Museum. Located in the Cour Napoleon, it sits between the Sully, Denon, and Richelieu wings. An urban legend holds that the pyramid has exactly 666 panes of glass, which is false. A smaller inverted pyramid also designed by Pei appears inside the museum. The structure was criticized by some for its stark contrast with the surrounding Baroque museum.

The Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in Cleveland, Ohio sits on the shores of Lake Erie. It features a glass 'tent' of two triangles leaning against a white rectangular tower, plus a roughly cylindrical segment supported by a single column rising from the water on the lakeshore side. The building includes a first-floor stage and a third-floor theater for performances and houses exhibits on artists such as The Beatles and Elvis Presley.

The East Building of the National Gallery of Art in Washington DC is Pei's prominent angular modernist addition alongside John Russell Pope's West Building (main gallery). The two buildings are connected by Leo Villareal's light sculpture Multiverse. The roof once displayed Katharina Fritsch's Hahn/Cock sculpture.

Other major works include the Museum of Islamic Art in Doha, Qatar, built on an offshore island; the Bank of China Tower in Hong Kong; the John F. Kennedy Presidential Library and Museum in Boston, which houses an enormous American flag; the Fragrant Hills Hotel outside Beijing (designed low-slung with white stucco walls and grey tiles rather than as a high-rise); Place Ville Marie in Montreal (with a white beacon atop and gateway to an underground mall); the NCAR Mesa Laboratory in Boulder, Colorado (Pueblo-inspired, initially criticized as 'just a bunch of towers'); the John Hancock Tower in Boston (designed by Henry N. Cobb of Pei's firm; notoriously suffered falling windowpanes in strong winds, earning the nickname 'Plywood Palace'); and a library in Columbus, Indiana with Henry Moore's Large Arch outside, built to mirror a nearby brick church's 166-foot tower. In the 1960s Pei drew up plans to redesign Oklahoma City's business districts, including the Myriad Botanical Gardens and Convention Center; local residents objected to the destruction of historic buildings such as the Criterion Theater.
